one of the things that I really started thinking deeply about yesterday as I was driving and listening to the book epicenter, was that it seems I have a lot more questions about gog and magog. There are a lot of refeences in different parts of the bible that I was unaware of. I really would like to do a deeper dive into this subject. Maybe even create a module just for this subject.
Also, I am very intrigued in the chapter that I am in now talking about the oil and gas beneath Isreal. Its crazy that its likely been there for YEARS but they recently found it. THE END TIMES ARE HERE
notes.n-m14:
03/31/2026 10:53 PM
🔗 DIRECT — The Abomination set up in the Holy Place
Matt 24:15: "When you see the abomination of desolation spoken of by Daniel the prophet, standing in the holy place (let the reader understand)..."
Rev 13:14–17: The Beast sets up his image; the False Prophet causes all to worship it. No one can buy or sell without the mark.
And again are these references the same

10:50 PM
You're right to push back on this one again. Let me think through it honestly:

Matt 24:15 is specifically about the abomination of desolation — the Antichrist entering the temple, stopping sacrifices, and setting up a sacrilegious object in the Holy Place. Jesus is pointing directly back to Daniel 9:27 and 11:31. This is a temple event at the midpoint of the tribulation.

Rev 13:14–17 is about the False Prophet creating an image of the Beast and forcing everyone to worship it, plus implementing the mark system for buying and selling. This is about global worship enforcement and economic control — it doesn't specifically mention the temple or the Holy Place.

Are they connected? Yes — they're both describing what happens after the Antichrist reveals himself at the midpoint. But they're not describing the same event. One is about desecrating the temple. The other is about a global worship and economic system. They happen in the same general timeframe but they're different actions.

A better Revelation cross-reference for Matt 24:15 would actually be 2 Thessalonians 2:4 — "He will even sit in the temple of God, claiming that he himself is God." That's the real direct parallel to the abomination in the Holy Place.

So this should be marked 〰️ THEMATIC — same era, related events, but not the same moment. 
______________________________________________________________________________________________
11:00 PM - SECTION 4
I'm trying to make reference to the Red Horse in War, and I and I do, I guess, kinda see that the Black Horse in Famine, I'm having a little bit of trouble with. Definitely get the pale horse, but I'm confused why it's a fourth of the Earth and not the entire Earth.10:57 PM1 / 2That's a sharp observation. Let me break down what you're noticing:
The black horse and famine — the balance scale in the rider's hand represents scarcity and rationing. A denarius was a day's wage, so paying that much for just a quart of wheat means food prices have skyrocketed. It's famine-level starvation, but the weird part is the voice says "do not harm the oil and the wine" — meaning some resources are still protected or abundant. So it's not total collapse everywhere, it's selective scarcity. That's why famine works as a symbol here — widespread hunger, but not uniformly.
The pale horse and "a fourth of the earth" — this is the one that trips people up. Death is given authority over only a quarter, not the whole world. A few interpretations:

Phased judgment — God's wrath is escalating. Seal 1–4 kill a quarter. Seals 5–6 intensify further. By the time you get to the trumpets and bowls, the death toll is much higher. So "a fourth" is the beginning of judgment, not the final tally.
Geographical/political — some scholars think it means a quarter of the world's population or a specific geographic quadrant gets hit hardest first.
Restraint — it emphasizes that even in judgment, God is limiting death, not unleashing total annihilation. He's holding back.

The fact that it's not the whole earth is actually important — it shows God's sovereignty and mercy even in wrath.
